context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

| Activities                  | Percentage |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Markets and Marketization

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How are market institutions different from the state? What do you need to know about market institutions if you want to work with them? How can you work jointly within different types of organizations and what are the challenges you might face by doing that? How does marketization — the process of creating markets — happen and what mistakes should it avoid?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on this 700–800 word reflection for my development planning class, and I’m kinda stuck on how to organize the prompt. I think it’s about state vs market institutions, but idk how to connect that to marketization.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — you've identified the two main actors. Before thinking about organization, let's make sure the concepts are clear. In your own words, what do you think makes a market institution fundamentally different from the state? What does each one rely on to function?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the start of the assignment and needs orientation without being given the structure outright. I'll use a Socratic question to get them thinking about the core distinction the prompt is asking about, which is the foundation for everything else. I'll keep it short and let them do the thinking.

exchanges.student: I think the state uses authority/law, and market institutions rely more on exchange and prices? I’m not fully sure what “market institution” includes tho, like is it just firms or also rules/norms?
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track with authority vs. exchange — that's a meaningful distinction. Now, on your uncertainty: think about what the word 'institution' means beyond just organizations. What kinds of rules or norms make markets *work* in the first place? That might help you answer your own question about whether it's just firms or something broader.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid intuition about the state vs. market distinction. Their uncertainty about what 'market institution' includes is actually a productive one that the prompt implicitly asks them to address ('what do you need to know about market institutions if you want to work with them'). I'll affirm the direction and push them to think more carefully about what makes something an 'institution' rather than just giving them the definition.

exchanges.student: Ok yeah, so maybe market institutions are broader than firms — like contracts, property rights, trust, competition rules, maybe even banks? I’m guessing markets don’t just “exist” naturally then.
exchanges.tutor: That's a really important insight — markets don't just emerge spontaneously, they require infrastructure. Now connect that to the prompt: if markets have to be *built*, what do you think can go wrong when someone tries to create a market where one didn't exist before?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a key insight — that markets don't just exist naturally but require supporting institutions. This directly connects to the marketization question in the prompt. I should affirm this and push them to follow the logical thread: if markets need to be constructed, what does that imply about how marketization happens and what can go wrong?

exchanges.student: Maybe ppl assume privatizing something automatically makes it efficient, but if the rules/regulation aren’t there it can get messy. I’m kinda fuzzy on specific mistakes tho, ngl.
exchanges.tutor: You already have the tools to figure this out. You just said markets need contracts, property rights, trust, competition rules. So what happens if you privatize something but those things aren't in place yet? What specific problems could follow from *each* of those missing pieces?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a good intuition about privatization without proper institutional infrastructure. Rather than giving examples of mistakes, I should push them to reason from what they've already established — that markets need rules, trust, property rights, etc. — to figure out what 'mistakes' look like logically. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: I think then u could get corruption, monopolies, weak enforcement, and ppl not trusting the system, so richer actors might just capture everything. Is that the kind of thing I should use for the “mistakes to avoid” part?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, exactly — those are well-reasoned examples that follow directly from what you established about market infrastructure. You've got solid material for the marketization part. Now, the prompt also asks about *working with* market institutions and working *jointly* across different types of organizations. Have you thought about what that part means for a planner or development practitioner?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has independently reasoned to several concrete and valid examples of marketization mistakes. I should affirm this and now nudge them toward the other parts of the prompt they haven't addressed yet — specifically 'working jointly within different types of organizations' and 'what do you need to know about market institutions if you want to work with them.' We've spent good time on the conceptual foundation; time to widen the scope.

exchanges.student: Sort of, but this is where I get lost tbh. I think it means working w/ states, firms, NGOs, maybe communities together, but idk what I’m supposed to say beyond “coordination is hard.”
exchanges.tutor: You've got the right actors. Now push deeper: you already said states run on authority and markets on exchange. How do NGOs or communities operate compared to those? When organizations with *different logics* have to work together, what specific tensions do you think emerge?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student recognizes the multi-actor dimension but is stuck at a surface level. Rather than elaborating for them, I should push them to think about *why* coordination is hard specifically between these different types of organizations — drawing on what they already established about how each type operates differently (authority vs. exchange vs. mission-driven, etc.).

exchanges.student: Maybe NGOs care more about social goals and communities care about local needs/legitimacy, so they don’t always match profit goals or state timelines. So the tension is like different incentives, power gaps, and maybe accountability getting confusing?
exchanges.tutor: Good — different incentives, power imbalances, and unclear accountability are all real and well-reasoned tensions. You've now touched on most parts of the prompt. One piece still underdeveloped: 'what do you need to *know* about market institutions to work with them?' As a planner, what specific knowledge would you need before engaging a market actor?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has done well reasoning through the tensions between different organizational logics. This is good material for their reflection. We've now covered most of the prompt's components. I should check if they feel ready to start writing, and also briefly flag the one piece they haven't addressed much — 'what do you need to know about market institutions if you want to work with them' — which is more practical/strategic.

exchanges.student: I’d prob need to know their incentives, who has power, what rules they follow, and whether competition is actually fair. Maybe also who gets excluded or harmed by the market setup?
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid and complete set of considerations. You've now worked through all four parts of the prompt. At this point, I'd encourage you to stop and start drafting — you have the ideas. How are you thinking about organizing all of this into a 700–800 word piece?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all the major components of the prompt through our conversation. They have good raw material for their reflection piece. This is a good moment to push them to start writing rather than continuing to develop ideas verbally. Staying in discussion mode risks them over-preparing without producing text. I should redirect them toward integrating everything into a draft.

exchanges.student: I’m thinking intro, then state vs market, then what planners need to know, then joint work/challenges, then marketization mistakes. Does that sound too list-y, or is that fine for this kind of reflection?
exchanges.tutor: That's worth thinking about yourself — what does a *reflection* piece typically do differently than a structured report? Does the prompt suggest any natural connections between those sections that might make it feel less like a list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to evaluate their structure, which risks me doing the organizational work for them. I should redirect the question back to them — what does a 'reflection piece' demand versus a report or essay? They should reason about genre themselves. I won't validate or critique the structure directly.

exchanges.student: Yeah maybe I should make it one argument instead of separate answers. I think my main point could be that markets need institutions and power, so planners can’t treat them as neutral or automatic?
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong central argument — it threads through everything you've reasoned today. Now go write it. Come back if you get stuck on a specific part.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a strong and coherent central argument that ties together all the components of the prompt. This is a significant intellectual step. I should affirm it genuinely and push them to start writing. We've had a productive 9-exchange conversation and they have all the material they need. No more scaffolding is necessary — they should now produce text.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all ten exchanges. Every key idea — market infrastructure, marketization pitfalls, cross-sector tensions, planner knowledge requirements, and the central argument — was drawn out from the student through targeted questioning rather than being supplied by the tutor. The tutor consistently built on student progress, kept responses concise and interactive, maintained a warm and supportive tone, and successfully guided the student to a coherent thesis by the final turn. No deductions were warranted under any criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never provided near-submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them — all key ideas emerged from student responses prompted by open questions. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently acknowledged and built on each correct student step (e.g., affirming the authority/exchange distinction, the infrastructure insight, the tension analysis) and used diagnostic questions rather than method dumps when the student expressed confusion. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level feedback at Turn 9 by redirecting the student to consider what a reflection piece does differently from a structured report, and at Turn 10 by affirming the argumentative framing as a structural method. Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment misalignment, and no off-topic drift. Communication was consistently bite-sized, precise, and supportive throughout.
